title: Slang Deep Dive
primer:
Slang is a modern shading language developed at NVIDIA and now an open-source project under Khronos. It cross-compiles to GLSL, HLSL, SPIR-V, DXIL, CUDA, and Metal from a single source — and adds language features that neither GLSL nor HLSL provide natively.

The four Slang features this lesson covers:

  • Generics and interfaces — Slang has a Rust/Swift-style interface (trait) system. You can write a generic material interface and have multiple material types implement it without runtime polymorphism overhead.
  • Modules — Slang has an import system (import myModule;) that enables true shader library reuse across projects. GLSL has no equivalent; HLSL has limited #include-based sharing.
  • Automatic differentiation (auto-diff) — Slang can automatically generate the derivative of a function with respect to its inputs, enabling gradient-based optimization and neural rendering directly in shader code.
  • ParameterBlock — a Slang-specific binding abstraction that groups a shader's resources and uniform data into a single type-safe object, avoiding manual register assignment.

In this lesson you will study Slang code and write the GLSL equivalent that achieves the same effect — understanding what Slang abstracts away and what must be expressed manually in GLSL.

challenges.title: Interface dispatch → manual branching in GLSL
hints: Call shadeLambert() when u_materialType is 0, shadeEmissive() when it is 1. A simple if/else covers both cases.
The normal and lightDir are already computed for you in the template — just pass them to the right function.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Slang interfaces compile to static dispatch (monomorphization) — the compiler generates a specialized shader per material type with zero branching overhead. In GLSL you must write the branching yourself, and the driver may or may not optimize it away. For a small fixed set of material types this is fine; for larger material systems, ubershader branching overhead can be significant. Slang's approach is equivalent to C++ templates or Rust traits and is one of its biggest advantages over raw GLSL/HLSL.

hlsl.note: HLSL has no interface system; the closest equivalent is function overloading or a manual switch.

slang.note: Original Slang with interface generics — the IMaterial dispatch is monomorphized at compile time.

challenges.title: ParameterBlock → GLSL uniform block + sampler
hints: Members of the uniform block (viewProj, sunDirection, exposure) are accessed by their bare names in GLSL — no gScene prefix needed.
gScene.skyTex.Sample(gScene.skySampler, uv) becomes texture(u_skyTex, uv) in GLSL.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Slang's ParameterBlock<T> is a first-class binding abstraction: the compiler automatically assigns register slots and generates the correct descriptor set layout for whatever API you target (Vulkan, D3D12, Metal). In GLSL you manage bindings manually via layout qualifiers. This is the main ergonomic advantage of Slang for large codebases — you describe relationships between resources, not raw hardware binding points.

hlsl.note: HLSL equivalent using explicit cbuffer + separate texture/sampler registers.

slang.note: Original Slang with ParameterBlock — resources and constants grouped in one type.

challenges.title: Auto-diff: understand the derivative
hints: The derivative of sigmoid(x) is sigma(x) * (1 - sigma(x)) — call sigmoid() again inside the derivative function.
This is a classic calculus result: d/dx [1/(1+e^-x)] = e^-x / (1+e^-x)^2 = s(x)(1-s(x)).
challenges.unlocks_theory: Slang's automatic differentiation generates exact analytic derivatives at compile time — the same result you would get by applying calculus rules by hand, but without the error-prone manual work. It supports both forward-mode (JVP) and reverse-mode (VJP/backpropagation) differentiation. This is the foundation of neural radiance fields (NeRF) and differentiable rendering research. In GLSL you must derive and code all derivatives yourself; Slang makes gradient-based GPU programs as straightforward as writing the primal function.

hlsl.note: HLSL has no auto-diff; the derivative must be written manually, same as GLSL.

slang.note: Original Slang with [Differentiable] and __fwd_diff() for automatic JVP generation.
